title: The Effects of Capital Requirements on Good and Bad Risk-Taking
abstract: We study capital requirement regulation in a dynamic quantitative model in which nonfinancial firms, as well as households, hold deposits. A novel general equilibrium channel that operates through firms deposits mitigates the cost of increasing capital requirements. In the calibrated model, (a) the optimal capital requirement is 7.3 percentage points higher than in a comparable model in which all the deposits are held by households, and (b) setting the capital requirement higher than the true optimum is not as costly as one would gauge from the comparable model. We also provide some independent evidence that supports our novel channel.

author.N Aaron Pancost: McCombs School of Business,University of Texas at Austin,USA
author.Roberto Robatto*: Wisconsin School of Business, University of Wisconsin-Madison,USA

article.1. Literature Review:
JEL E20 - General G21 - Banks; Depository Institutions; Micro Finance Institutions; Mortgages G32 - Financing Policy; Financial Risk and Risk Management; Capital and Ownership Structure; Value of Firms; Goodwill Issue Section: Article Editor: Itay Goldstein Itay Goldstein Editor Search for other works by this author on: Oxford Academic Google Scholar

After the 2007–2008 financial crisis, the revision of the regulatory framework of financial intermediaries has become a central topic of discussion by regulators and academics. The Basel III accord has tightened bank regulation with the aim of reducing the likelihood and depth of financial crises. One of the key sets of rules at the center of this debate is capital requirements, namely, limits on the fraction of debt that banks can use to finance their investment. Appealing to theModigliani and Miller (1958)theorem,Admati and Hellwig (2013)argue that capital requirements should be raised even further to eliminate banks’ bad risk-taking, namely, the moral hazard induced by government guarantees and implicit too-big-to-fail subsidies.1One argument against tighter capital requirements is that banks are special because their liabilities are valued for their safety and liquidity (DeAngelo and Stulz 2015). Under this view, raising capital requirements reduces excessive risky lending by banks but at the cost of reducing the supply of valuable bank deposits.

Recent literature has analyzed these trade-offs in a quantitative, general equilibrium framework. While most analyses use models in which all the deposits are held by households (Van den Heuvel 2008;Davydiuk 2017;Begenau 2020), deposits in practice are held not only by households but also by firms. As of 2010, the year in which Basel III was agreed on, U.S. firms accounted for about one-third of the deposits held by households and firms.

In this paper, we ask whether and to what extent accounting for the deposits held by nonfinancial firms affects the determination of the optimal capital requirement. We derive a quantitative, general equilibrium model that we calibrate to U.S. data. Similar to the literature, government guarantees induce banks to take excessive risks. Capital requirements limit this risk, but they also reduce the supply of deposits. Crucially, though, the cost of reducing the supply of deposits depends not only on how households value deposits but also on how the lower supply affects firms’ behavior.

Our main results are that (a) the optimal capital requirement is higher than in comparable models in which all the deposits are held by households, and (b) setting the capital requirement higher than the true optimum is not as costly as one would gauge from the comparable models. In our calibrated model, the optimal capital requirement is 21.9%, and only 14.6% in a comparable model in which households hold all the deposits. With respect to item (b), setting capital requirements 10 percentage points above the optimum erases almost all the welfare gains in the model in which all the deposits are held by households. But in the full model in which some of the deposits are held by firms, setting the capital requirement 10 percentage points above the optimal value delivers almost 90%of the welfare gains that are achieved at the optimum.

In our model, firms are subject to idiosyncratic shocks that lower their demand for production inputs—particularly labor—relative to an economy without such shocks (Quadrini 2017). Because deposits are safe, they implicitly provide insurance by reducing the volatility of firms’ cash flows, so that a high availability of deposits increases firms’ willingness to engage in risky production and hire workers.2We call this channelgood risk-takingbecause increasing labor demand is socially valuable, but not fully exploited because of the idiosyncratic risk.3Deposits are also held by households, who derive utility from them as inVan den Heuvel (2008).

Our main result follows from the fact that, from the regulator’s point of view, the benefit of deposits that arises from their safety and liquidity differs depending on whether deposits are held by households or firms: for every dollar of deposits, the value is much higher when the owner is a household. As a result, an increase in capital requirements that reduces the supply of deposits is not that detrimental for welfare in our model—in which some deposits are held by firms—in comparison to a model with the same total level of deposits which are all held by households.

Deposits held by firms are less valuable from the regulator’s point of view because of a general equilibrium effect that works through firms’ input prices, and in particular, wages. Tightening capital requirements reduces deposits and, thus, firms’ labor demand. At constant wages, the reduction in labor demand would lead to a large decline in production and welfare. However, if labor supply is relatively inelastic, the equilibrium quantities of labor and production only fall by a little, and instead falling wages absorb most of the drop in demand. The lower wages do not affect welfare because households are both workers and firms’ shareholders, and the reduction in wages translates into higher firms’ profits. Thus, the aggregate costs of reducing liquidity supply in this model are lower than in a comparable one with the same level of deposits, but in which households hold all deposits and bear the entire cost of the policy change.

We also provide reduced-form evidence supporting the good risk-taking channel (i.e., the mechanism by which a higher availability of deposits affects employment and wages) using two different approaches that build onJayaratne and Strahan (1996)andDrechsler, Savov, and Schnabl (2017), respectively. These analyses complement the evidence inBenmelech, Bergman, and Seru (2021), who show that firms’ employment decisions are significantly affected by firms’ liquidity.

Another contribution of the paper is the way we model the link between capital requirements and banks’ bad risk-taking. To match the large spikes in banks’ default rates in practice, we allow banks in our model to engage in endogenous and socially inefficient risk-shifting (Jensen and Meckling, 1976), which can be curbed by tighter capital requirements.4Our formulation allows us to distinguish between total and risk-weighted assets, so that capital requirements are imposed on the latter, as is the case in practice. In addition, it matches the fact that an increase in the riskiness of banks’ assets (measured by the ratio of risk-weighted to total assets) is followed on average by an increase in banks’ default rates 1-year ahead.

Other aspects of our work build on the existing literature that quantifies optimal capital requirements. We follow the pioneering approach ofVan den Heuvel (2008)in embedding the analysis of capital requirements regulation in a dynamic, quantitative, general equilibrium model, and some elements of our analysis are based onVan den Heuvel (2008),Davydiuk (2017), andBegenau (2020).5

Capital requirements in our model limit the negative impact of subsidized deposit insurance. More generally, though, deposit insurance can also be interpreted as any explicit or implicit government guarantee on bank debt (e.g., the Temporary Liquidity Guarantee Program set up by the Federal Deposit Insurance Corporation in 2008 and the too-big-to-fail guarantee that resulted in the Paulson plan studied byVeronesi and Zingales 2010). We follow a common approach in the literature that imposes deposit insurance and motivates it with its role in preventing runs, as inDiamond and Dybvig (1983), but does not explicitly include runs or analyze the optimality of such a policy.6We note that government guarantees and capital requirements are important not only for households’ but also for firms’ deposits. Indeed, even if some firms might have high deposit balances, the government has been covering deposits and other bank liabilities well beyond the deposit insurance limit.7

We motivate firms’ aversion to idiosyncratic risk with an agency problem, following a literature that has highlighted the importance of these frictions (Panousi and Papanikolaou 2012;Glover and Levine 2015;Glover and Levine 2017). Firms are owned by households that can fully diversify away their exposure to idiosyncratic risk by holding a well-diversified portfolio of equity. However, each firm is run by a manager who holds only an indifferentiated stake in the firm she manages and thus is exposed to the idiosyncratic risk of the firm. We introduce these features building onQuadrini (2017)and, as an additional contribution of the paper, we use some modeling assumptions that make the model tractable without altering the implications of the agency friction. In particular, managers’ consumption does not affect the welfare analysis, and the firms’ building block of the model can be aggregated easily. That is, even if idiosyncratic shocks create heterogeneity across firms, the equilibrium in our model depends on aggregate firm wealth only, and other moments of the firm size distribution are irrelevant. This allows us to maintain the focus on financial regulation and to solve the model in general equilibrium. This last feature is particularly important for our novel channel; richer structural corporate finance models with agency frictions, such asNikolov and Whited (2014), are instead typically solved in partial equilibrium.
A recent and growing literature has embedded the analysis of capital requirements into quantitative general equilibrium models. This literature includesVan den Heuvel (2008),Christiano and Ikeda (2013),Nguyễn (2014),Martinez-Miera and Suarez (2014),Gertler, Kiyotaki, and Prestipino (2016),Begenau and Landvoigt (2021),Davydiuk (2017),Dempsey (2017),Begenau (2020), andElenev, Landvoigt, and Van Nieuwerburgh (2021).8Most of the literature, however, tends to be qualitative or to abstract from general equilibrium (Thakor 2014). Indeed, several papers, such asBerger and Bouwman (2013),Mehran and Thakor (2011), andDiamond and Rajan (2001), provide results about the role of bank capital and changes in capital requirements that are identified in the cross-section, abstracting from general equilibrium effects.

A related paper byAllen et al. (2018)studies the effect of government guarantees on banks’ choices of liquidity and investments in risky projects. Both our paper and that ofAllen et al. (2018)point out that higher risk-taking can be a good consequence of financial regulation. In their paper, more risk-taking by banks is associated with greater liquidity provision. In our paper, more risk-taking by firms has a positive impact, in general, on employment and output.

This paper is also related to a broader banking literature that conducts policy analyses in models in which only households hold safe deposits, such asStein (2012). While those theoretical results are not affected by our novel channel, the corresponding quantitative policy stance might need to be tilted by imposing stricter limits on intermediaries.

Our paper relates to a large literature on firms’ exposure to idiosyncratic risks.Smith and Stulz (1985)andFroot, Scharfstein, and Stein (1993)derive theories in which firms do not completely hedge away idiosyncratic risks because external financing is costly.Minton and Schrand (1999)provide evidence that firms that are more exposed to idiosyncratic risk invest less and have higher costs of external financing than other firms, consistent with an inability or unwillingness to completely insure against idiosyncratic shocks.Panousi and Papanikolaou (2012)show that risk aversion of managers and their exposure to firms’ idiosyncratic risk plays a role in shaping firms’ decisions.
article.2. Intuition for Our Main Channel:
In this section, we provide intuition behind the novel model mechanism that delivers our main result: relative to a model in which all the bank deposits are held by households, a model in which a fraction of the deposits are held by firms leads to a higher optimal capital requirement. We clarify our argument by breaking it down in several simple steps and analyzing each one in detail.

Consider first a baseline model (such asVan den Heuvel 2008) in which deposits enter the utility function of the representative household. In the literature, the parameters that govern households’ utility from deposits are typically calibrated to match the observed liquidity premium on deposits, which is the difference between the return on deposits and the return on less liquid but still riskless assets. Second, consider an alternative model that includes a new class of deposit-holding agents (i.e., firms) but in which the total amount of deposits in the economy and the observed deposit premium are the same. In this new model, only a fraction (say,X%⁠) of the observed deposit premium is due to the household’s direct utility for deposits; the remaining fraction (100-X%⁠) comes from firms’ demand for deposits. In our model, firms use deposits to lessen the negative impact of uninsurable idiosyncratic shocks. In particular, a higher availability of deposits reduces the volatility of firms’ assets and cash-flows. This, in turn, encourages firms to increase their production and, in particular, hire more workers.

Next, consider the effects of raising capital requirements, which reduces the quantity of deposits, in both models. In the first model, in which households hold 100%of deposits, the reduction in deposit is fully absorbed by households and reduces their utility, which ultimately lowers welfare. As shown byVan den Heuvel (2008), the welfare effect depends on the liquidity premium (and a few other observables), and is higher the higher the premium is to start with. In the second model, in which some of the deposits are held by firms, however, the reduction in deposits is absorbed in part by households and in part by firms. With lower deposits, firms reduce their labor demand according to the good risk-taking channel explained before. The key insight is that while a reduction in deposit supply reduces firmsdemandfor labor, what matters for welfare is what happens to theequilibriumquantity of labor, which depends also on laborsupply. In particular, theelasticityof labor supply crucially affects the outcome.Figure 1clarifies this result by considering two extreme cases.

Figure 1 Equilibrium in the labor market and effects of a change in labor demand Left panel, infinitely elastic labor supply; right panel, fully inelastic labor supply. Open in new tab Download slide

The left panel ofFigure 1depicts a completely elastic labor supply. In this case, wages are not affected by a change in demand, and a reduction in labor supply leads to a one-for-one reduction in employment, which reduces welfare, in line with the outcome of the model with only households’ deposits.

The right panel ofFigure 1depicts the other extreme case: a fully rigid labor supply (i.e., the elasticity of labor supply is zero). A reduction in labor demand does not affect employment, and instead only lowers wages. However, the reduction in wages leads to higher firms’ profits, and the two effects cancel out as long as workers are also the owners of firms—which will be the case in our model. As a result, the100−X%of the liquidity premium due to firms’ deposits produces no welfare consequences through the labor market.9The logic ofVan den Heuvel (2008)still applies to theX%of the deposit premium which is due to households’ deposits. But the welfare costs of higher capital requirements are lower than in the case in which 100%of the deposit premium is due to households deposits. As a result, the optimal capital requirement is higher. Notice also that the fall in wages need not be large—if firms’ labor demand is very elastic, a modest change in wages is sufficient to keep employment constant.

In sum, the extent to which firm deposits affect welfare thus depends on the Frisch elasticity of labor supply. The recent micro- and macro-labor literature suggests that the Frisch elasticity is quite low, and we use similar values in our calibrated model. Hence, the welfare effects of raising capital requirements that arise through the labor market are modest. Our estimates are also conservative in that we assume a high degree of frictions in the labor market that prevent wages from responding quickly to shocks.

Absent from this discussion, by design, is any consideration of the benefits of increased capital requirements, because we take them to be the same in the two models. We model these benefits as arising from a reduction in banks’ risk-taking behavior at higher capital requirements (what we call “bad” risk-taking). Taken together, we argue that a higher capital requirement in an economy with a somewhat-inelastic labor supply will reduce banks’ bad risk-taking—which is good for welfare—with only a limited impact on employment, because falling wages absorb most of the drop in labor demand. As a result, a regulator can set a much higher capital requirement, relative to a model in which 100%of the deposit premium comes from households’ utility for deposits. The model that we will present next shows that this argument is quantitatively relevant in practice.
article.3. Quantitative Model:
We now present a dynamic general equilibrium model that we then use to conduct a quantitative analysis and to determine the optimal capital requirement. Time is discrete and infinite. There are four players in the economy: firms, banks, households, and the government.

We followQuadrini (2017)in assuming that firms are subject to idiosyncratic risk and hold deposits for precautionary reasons, but we depart significantly from his model. In particular, each firm is run by a risk-averse manager who acts in her own interest because of an agency friction. This friction motivates the manager’s holding of an undifferentiated stake in the firm she runs and, thus, an exposure to firm-specific idiosyncratic risk (Panousi and Papanikolaou 2012;Glover and Levine 2015;Glover and Levine 2017). In contrast, firms’ owners (i.e., households) fully diversify away the idiosyncratic risk by holding equity stakes in all firms. The firms’ side of the model aggregates up to a “representative firm”—even though firms are heterogeneous because of the idiosyncratic shocks—keeping the model tractable.

Banks collect deposits from firms and households, provide loans to firms, and are subject to idiosyncratic shocks that make a fraction of them insolvent. Deposits at insolvent banks are made whole by the government through deposit insurance.

Households consume, supply labor and bank equity, and own firms and banks. We will impose some parameter restrictions so that the objective of financial regulation will be solely that of maximizing the welfare of households, without any consideration for the welfare of managers.

The manager’s compensation depends on a contract that includes a fixed component, an equity stake, and an option.10The manager makes all the firms’ choices other than the decision about dividends, which is instead controlled by the shareholders (i.e., the households). We return to the motivation for this assumption when presenting the household side of the model, in Section3.3.

The results of Proposition 1 imply that our model is consistent with some relevant evidence. First, as inOpler et al. (1999), (a) riskier cash flow (i.e., higher variance ofzt+1i⁠) implies that the firm is willing to hold more cash, and (b) firms that do well (i.e., firms hit by a sequence of good values ofzt+1i⁠) accumulate cash internally, and vice versa. Second, as firms accumulate cash, they increase their labor force, in line with the evidence ofBenmelech, Bergman, and Seru (2021)about the effect of cash held by the firm on the firm’s labor force. Third, lower cash flow volatility increases investments, along the line of the evidence inMinton and Schrand (1999), and because capital and labor are complementary in production, increases labor demand too.

3.2 Banks

Banks live for a single period: each bank is set up at timetand is liquidated at the beginning of timet+1⁠.16Each newly created bank receives an amountntof net worth from its shareholders (i.e., households). Then, the bank collects depositsdtfrom firms and households, subject to a capital requirement constraint, and uses the resourcesnt+dtto purchase physical capitalbt⁠, which represent loans to firms. Profits are bounded below by a limited liability constraint, and when a bank fails, it is taken over and liquidated by the government. Deposits are protected and fully repaid because of the deposit insurance.

Banks are subject to idiosyncratic shocks that affect their cash flow att+1⁠. These shocks capture the fact that some banks experience higher or lower losses on their loans and investments in comparison to the average bank in the economy. We assume a baseline level of exogenous risk that banks cannot get rid of, so that some banks will always fail each period.

In addition to the baseline level of idiosyncratic risk, banks can engage in risk shifting (Jensen and Meckling 1976;Repullo 2004) by increasing the volatility of their idiosyncratic shock, at a cost.17We add the risk-shifting element to the model for quantitative reasons—without, it the model fails to reproduce the large spikes in banks’ default rates during crisis periods, although our main qualitative results continue to hold as we show in Section4.5. Other papers use a similar approach to improve the quantitative performance of their models. For instance, inBegenau (2020), banks can choose a suboptimal level of monitoring to increase the bailout subsidy they receive from the government.

To clarify the mechanics of the model, we note that limited liability and deposit insurance imply that banks have an incentive to increase their exposure to idiosyncratic risk. They can do so in two ways: (1) by increasing the size of their balance sheet and (2) by engaging in risk shifting. We call this behavior bad risk-taking because although it is privately optimal for banks, it is socially wasteful: banks reap the profits if their realized idiosyncratic shock is high, but pass the costs on to the government if the realization is low. Two related remarks are important. First, risk shifting creates value for individual banks but is socially costly because engaging in it comes at a real cost. Hence, the socially optimal amount of risk shifting is zero. Second, even without risk shifting, banks can take advantage of the deposit insurance subsidy by expanding their balance sheet beyond what would be socially optimal.

To describe the problem of banks, consider first the case in which banks do not to engage in risk shifting. Each bank lends its physical capitalbtto the firms, and after production takes place att+1⁠, firms return the undepreciated fraction1−δplus a returnrt⁠. The resourcesbt[1−δ+rt]returned by firms are then hit by the idiosyncratic shockε⁠, withE(ε)=1⁠. Thus, banks’ profits att+1are given by the cash flowεbt[1−δ+rt]net of the repaymentRtddtto depositors, whereRtdis the gross return on deposits.

The full problem of banks includes the choice of risk shifting, which we denote by p t ≥ 0 ⁠ , in addition to that of loans b t and deposits d t ⁠ : max b t , d t , p t ≥ 0 E t ∫ ε ― t ∞ { ε b t [ 1 − δ + r t − λ ( p t ) r t ] − R t d d t } d F ( ε ; p t ) , (15) subject to the budget and capital requirement constraints: b t = d t + n t , (16) n t ≥ ζ b t 1 − ν p t , (17) where ζ is the capital requirement, ν ≥ 0 parameterizes the dependence of the capital requirement constraint on the risk-shifting choice p t (discussed below), and ε ― t is the threshold of the idiosyncratic shock ε below which a bank defaults. 18

The risk-shifting choicepthas two effects on the bank’s profits. First,ptaffects the distribution of the idiosyncratic shockF(ε;pt)⁠. In particular, we assume that the variance ofεis increasing inptbut the mean is not affected, that is,E(ε)=1for allpt⁠. With higher variance, banks’ default rate goes up, increasing the reliance on deposit insurance and, as a result, the deposit insurance subsidy. This effect increases expected bank profits. Second, a choicept>0is costly, in the sense that it reduces the return on bank profits byλ(pt)rt⁠. Banks trade off these two effects when settingptto maximize their expected profits. We assumeλ(0)=0andλ′(p)>0⁠, and we specify the functional forms ofF(⋅)andλ(⋅)when calibrating the model, in Section4.1. In particular, under our parameterization, the risk-shifting constraintpt≥0will be occasionally binding. As a result, the model will produce “normal times” with low default rates (i.e.,pt=0⁠) and “crisis times” with spikes in the default rate (i.e.,pt>0⁠), as in the data.

In addition to bank’s profits, the risk-shifting choiceptaffects the capital requirement constraint (17): as banks engage in riskier activities, their capital requirement constraint tightens. This effect mirrors how banks are regulated in practice: starting with Basel I, capital requirements are applied to risk-weighted assets as opposed to total assets. Beyond risk-weighted capital requirements, banks are subject to additional regulatory scrutiny, such as inspections and CAMELS ratings, which also affect the riskiness of their loans (Rezende and Wu 2013;Gopalan, Kalda, and Manela 2021;Hirtle, Kovner, and Plosser 2020). We model the link betweenpt⁠, risk-weighted capital, and the capital requirement with the parameterν⁠, which we discipline in the calibration by interpreting the termbt/(1−νpt)on the right-hand side of (17) as risk-weighted assets.

Accounting for the endogenous reaction of regulation to bad risk-taking is necessary to produce an accurate policy analysis. For example, a regulatory constraint that does not account forpt⁠, such as one of the formnt≥ζbt⁠, would imply that a reduction inζwould lead banks to massively increasept⁠, with very negative consequences on welfare. In practice, however, risk-weights, inspections, and CAMELS ratings attenuate this effect, and our formulation captures this outcome. In other words, omittingptfrom (17) would overestimate the welfare impact of changing capital requirements.

The maximization problem (15) requires solving for the size of the banking sector,bt⁠, and the amount of risk shiftingpt⁠. Here, we discuss solving forbt⁠, and we postpone the discussion ofptto Section4.1, after we specify the functional forms forF(⋅)andλ(⋅)⁠.

To solve for the banks’ size, we note that the capital requirement constraint ( 17 ) is always binding in equilibrium because the return on deposits will be less than the return on banks’ capital. Given n t (and p t ⁠ ), Equation ( 17 ) pins down the size b t of banks’ assets, and then the budget constraint ( 16 ) yields the value of deposits d t ⁠ . Finally, the equilibrium value of n t is determined by households (described in Section 3.3 ), given the return on equity R t + 1 n ⁠ . Because households can diversify their holdings across banks, Equation ( 15 ) implies that R t + 1 n is given by R t + 1 n = 1 n t ∫ ε ― t ∞ { ε b t [ 1 − δ + r t − λ ( p t ) r t ] − R t d d t } d F ( ε ; p t ) . 3.3 Households

Households are infinitely lived agents that enjoy utility from consumptionctand disutility of laborlt⁠. In addition, we followVan den Heuvel (2008),Begenau (2020), and several other papers in the capital requirements literature by assuming that households enjoy utility from holding depositsdth⁠, a feature that captures the liquidity and transaction benefits that are provided by bank deposits above and beyond their pecuniary return.19As mentioned before, households are owners of both firms and banks and thus receive firms’ dividends and the return on bank equity.

Households’ utility is given by ∑ t = 0 ∞ β t [ c t 1 − γ m − 1 1 − γ m + ψ ( d t h / c t ) 1 − γ d 1 − γ d − β χ l t 1 + 1 η 1 + 1 η ] , (19) where β ∈ ( 0 , 1 ) is the subjective discount factor, γ m > 0 is the coefficient of relative risk aversion, γ d > 0 and ψ ≥ 0 govern the demand of deposits, and χ > 0 and η > 0 govern the disutility of labor (and thus, labor supply). Households choose labor at time t but do not provide that labor until the beginning of period t + 1 ⁠ , when production actually occurs; thus, we discount the disutility of labor chosen at t with an additional β in Equation ( 19 ). We postpone the discussion of labor supply and the labor market to Section 3.4 , so that this section illustrates the other households’ choices taking l t as given.

Households choose consumption, investments in bank equity, deposits, and—in their role as firms’ shareholders—the firms’ dividend policy (i.e., the fractionαtiof firms’ wealth that is paid out). The assumption that dividends are chosen by households—as opposed to managers—is motivated by the results ofLa Porta et al. (2000). They find that in countries with good legal protections for shareholders, such as the United States, firms choose dividend policies that are consistent with shareholders’ rather than managers’ preferences.20

(21)

At time t ⁠ , the household chooses how to allocate its wealth a t h between consumption c t ⁠ , deposits d t h ⁠ , and investment in bank equity n t ⁠ . Wealth at t + 1 is given by a t + 1 h = w t l t + n t R t + 1 n + d t h R t d + π t + 1 f − T t + 1 . (22)

That is,at+1his the sum of labor incomewtlt⁠, the gross return on banks’ equityntRt+1nand depositsdthRtd⁠, and the profitsπt+1fdistributed by firms, net of lump-sum taxesTt+1⁠.

In addition to consumption and investment, households also choose the dividend policy of firms,αtifor alli(see Section3.1). They do so to maximize their valuation of each firm, balancing the benefits of leaving cash in the firm—which provides insurance to managers’ exposure to idiosyncratic risk and thus increases good risk-taking—with the value of an additional dollar of dividends.

The next proposition summarizes the household’s optimal choices of consumption, investment, and firms’ dividends, focusing on the relevant case that we will analyze when solving the model in which the parameterκthat governs the manager’s compensation is small.

To understand (26), consider the cost and benefit of reducing firms’ dividends so that each firm in the economy holds an additional dollar. The cost, on the left-hand side, is given by the deposit premium (i.e., the fact that the returnRdon deposits is less than the risk-free illiquid return, which is1/βin the nonstochastic steady state). Crucially, the right-hand side of (26) represents theprivatebenefit of firms’ holdings of deposits because it is computed by taking prices as given—in particular, the wage. 3.4 Labor market

The labor market is competitive, but wages are rigid in the sense that they adjust slowly over time in response to shocks. Our formulation for wage rigidities mirrors those derived in the macro-labor literature that microfounds such rigidities using infrequent bargaining between workers and firms (Gertler and Trigari 2009;Gertler, Huckfeldt, and Trigari 2020).

Let w t f be the frictionless level of the wage that would prevail without wage rigidities. We assume that the actual wage w t is given by w t = ω w t − 1 + ( 1 − ω ) w t f , (27) where ω ∈ [ 0 , 1 ) parametrizes the degree of wage rigidity. The model nests a standard case with fully flexible wages (i.e., ω = 0 ⁠ ) as well as a case in which the wage rigidity is extreme (i.e., ω → 1 ⁠ ). Given w t ⁠ , the amount of labor l t is determined by firms’ demand, similar to Shimer (2012) . The frictionless wage w t f is defined to be the one that would satisfy the first-order condition of the households in a frictionless model: w t f E t { Λ t + 1 } = χ l t 1 / η . (28)

Equation (28) is similar to a standard labor supply condition, with the difference that it depends on the marginal utility of consumption att+1⁠, (i.e.,Λt+1⁠) rather than att⁠. This is because, in our model, firms hire workers at timetbut pay the wages att+1⁠.

Because our main channel operates through the adjustment of wages following a change in capital requirements, the wage rigidities in Equation (27) dampen our main channel and make the analysis more conservative. In particular, the short-term dynamics of the wagewtare crucially affected by the rigidity parameterω⁠: at the time of a change in capital requirementsζ⁠, a high value ofωimplies that wages do not absorb as much of the shock, leading to a larger fall in labor—and thus lower welfare—than would obtain in a frictionless model. This dampening effect ofωthus works against any inelasticity in labor supply coming from a low value of the Frisch elasticityη⁠, as discussed in Section2. On the other hand, wage rigidities do not play any role in steady-state (i.e., the long run), in which equilibrium in the labor market is determined by the interaction of firms’ labor demand and households’ labor supply—in line with the modeling approach used in the macro-labor literature.

3.5 Government

The government taxes households to ensure that depositors at failed banks are made whole. Att+1the government seizes output at failed banks (who return zero to their equity holders) in order to partially defray the expenses of paying back depositors.

The total amount of tax to be collected is T t + 1 = ∫ − ∞ ε ― t [ R t d d t − ε b t [ 1 − δ + r t − λ ( p t ) r t ] ] d F ( ε ; p t ) , (29) where R t d d t is the amount owed to depositors, and the term ε b t [ 1 − δ + r t − λ ( p t ) r t ] for ε < ε ― t denotes the assets of failed banks.

We assume that the productivity of firms, A t ⁠ , follows an AR(1) process in logarithms. The law of motion of A t is log ⁡ A t = ( 1 − ρ ) log ⁡ A ¯ + ρ log ⁡ A t − 1 + σ A ε t A , where A ¯ , σ A > 0 and ε t A ∼ N ( 0 , 1 ) ⁠ .

Given an initial conditions for aggregate firm assetsa0f⁠, household wealtha0h⁠, and the exogenous stochastic processes forAt⁠,{zti}and{ε}⁠, an equilibrium is a collection of firm policies, bank policies, household policies, government taxes, wages, and return on deposits such that: managers maximize their expected utility (8); banks maximizes their expected profits (15); households maximize their expected utility (19); the government budget constraint (29) holds; the wagewtis determined by Equations (27) and (28); and the return on depositsRtdclears the deposit market.

The aggregate resource constraint will hold by Walras’ law. Nonetheless, we state it to clarify the mechanics of the model: c t + ( k t + b t ) ≤ ( 1 − δ ) ( k t − 1 + b t − 1 ) + A t ( k t − 1 + b t − 1 ) γ l t − 1 1 − γ − λ ( p t − 1 ) r t − 1 b t − 1 − φ 2 x t ( α t − α ¯ ) 2 .

The resources available to the economy, on the right-hand side, consist of the undepreciated capital plus output (recall that output produced by firms depends on choices made att−1⁠) and net of the resources that are wasted because of risk shifting and the dividend adjustment costs.21The left-hand side states that these resources can be consumed or invested in capital.

3.8 Welfare

When computing optimal capital requirements, we should, in principle, account for the welfare of both types of agents in the economy: households and managers. However, doing so requires taking a stand on the weight to be assigned to each type in the welfare function—a task that would significantly complicate the analysis. To address this issue, we note that the fraction of managers in the population is small relative to households, and their compensation is small relative to firms’ profits. In this section, we formalize the notion that total consumption and total welfare are well approximated by households’ consumption and welfare, respectively, so that we can compute the optimal capital requirement by looking solely at the welfare of households.

In general, the welfare function W is the sum of households’ and managers’ welfare: W = V 0 h ( a 0 h ) + θ ∫ V 0 m ( a 0 i ) d i , (30) where θ > 0 is a Pareto weight that defines managers’ contribution to total welfare in comparison to households. The welfare analysis depends, in principle, on both the Pareto weight θ and the parameter κ that scales the compensation of managers. We discipline these two parameters in such a way that (a) welfare depends only on households’ utility, W = V 0 h ( a 0 ) ⁠ ; (b) aggregate consumption depends only on households’ consumption; and (c) the implication of the agency friction and the easy aggregation of the firms’ side of the model continue to hold.

To disciplineθ⁠, we note that the fraction of managers in the population is, in practice, small. As a result, we considerθto be arbitrarily small so that the economywide welfare depends only on households’ utility:W=V0h(a0)⁠. InInternet Appendix D, we provide a simple extension of the model in which we derive the resultW=V0h(a0)even if we set the Pareto weightθto one (i.e., even if each manager has the same Pareto weight as each household). To disciplineκ⁠, we note that managers’ compensation are small relative to the overall dividends paid by firms and thus we setκ≈0⁠.22We formalize these choices and their implications in the next proposition.

Proposition 3 (Parameter restrictions and welfare) If κ → 0 and θ → 0 (with θ proportional to κ γ m along the limiting sequence), manager i ’s optimal choices in Proposition 1 are not affected, manager i ’s consumption converges to c t + 1 i → 0 ⁠ , profits distributed to households become π t + 1 f → α t + 1 x t + 1 and the economywide welfare function W becomes equal to the value function of households, W → V 0 h ( a 0 ) ⁠ .

The key result of Proposition 3 is that the importance of managers vanishes for welfare purposes (i.e., their consumption converges to zero) without affecting their decisions. As a result, we can just focus on the welfare of households when evaluating financial regulation, even though the model exhibits a behavior driven by an agency friction.
article.4. Calibration and Quantitative Analysis:
We now take the model to the data. To gauge the impact of our novel channel on the optimal capital requirement, we provide two calibrations: a benchmark calibration in which both households and firms hold deposits, and a restricted calibration in which all the deposits are held by households. More precisely, the restricted calibration features no idiosyncratic risk for firms, so that firms have no reason to hold deposits. We match both calibrations to the same target moments—the full calibration, since it has more parameters, also targets a few more moments—and compute the optimal capital requirement for each calibration.

We calibrate the model to annual U.S. data from 1986 to 2010. This choice maximizes the length of the data sample, while focusing on a period of time in which (a) banks were allowed to pay interest on most deposit instruments and (b) there was a single capital requirement regime in the sample. The majority of Regulation Q restrictions on interest rates were phased out between 1980 and 1985 (Gilbert 1986), and capital regulation throughout most of our sample was determined by Basel I, with the switch to the Basel III agreement and the Dodd-Frank Act signed in 2010.

We solve the model using nonlinear global projection methods that allow for rich dynamics and occasionally binding constraints (seeInternet Appendix Gfor more details). In particular, the constraint that is occasionally binding is the one that determines banks’ risk shifting, that is,pt≥0(see Equation15). As discussed in Section3.2, risk shifting allows banks to increase their exposure to idiosyncratic risk to exploit the implicit deposit insurance subsidy. This feature of the model allows us to match both the spikes in bank failures during crisis times and the long periods with few bank failures.

We begin by specifying the functional forms that govern banks’ shocks and risk shifting (Section4.1) and then turn to calibrating parameter values (Section4.2). Starting with an8%capital requirement, we simulate the model 2,500 times for 25 periods — so that the number of periods corresponds to the number of years in our sample — and then show how various untargeted moments in the model compare to the data (Section4.3).23Finally, we study the effects of changing the capital requirement in the baseline calibration (Section4.4) and under several alternative calibrations (Section4.5).

4.1 Banks’ bad risk-taking: Functional forms

Before calibrating the model, we specify the functional forms that govern banks’ idiosyncratic shocks and risk-shifting decisions. Recall from Section3.2the two functions that we need to specify: the functionλ(pt)that parameterizes the costs associated with the risk-shifting choicept⁠, and the cumulative distribution function (CDF)F(ε;pt)that governs the banks’ idiosyncratic shocksε⁠.

We begin by discussing the choice of the function λ ( p t ) ⁠ . In principle, we do not know the exact functional form, and making wrong assumptions could affect the policy analysis. However, we exploit the fact that any (well-behaved) function can be approximated arbitrarily well by a polynomial function (i.e., the Stone-Weierstrass approximation theorem). Thus, we can write λ ( p t ) ≈ ∑ j = 0 J λ j p t j for some J ≥ 0 ⁠ . We choose J = 2 ⁠ , so that we are taking a second-order Taylor approximation of the true λ ( p t ) function. Thus, we have λ ( p t ) = λ 0 + λ 1 p t + λ 2 p t 2 .

We setλ0=0⁠, so thatλ(0)=0(i.e., there are no costs associated with choosingpt=0⁠), and we calibrateλ1andλ2in Section4.2to match some key moments of the default rate distribution in the data.

Next, we turn to the idiosyncratic shock ε and its CDF F ( ε , p t ) ⁠ . We assume that the idiosyncratic shock ε is ε ∼ { 0 with probability p t ε ~ 1 − p t with probability 1 − p t , (31) where ε ~ is a lognormal random variable with variance σ and E { ε ~ } = 1 ⁠ . Note that ( 31 ) implies, by construction, that E { ε } = 1 for all values of p t ⁠ . The need to have a tractable specification in our rich model plays a role in selecting this functional form, but we show in Table 2 in Section 4.3 that the model matches several untargeted moments related to banks’ choices and default.

Panel A ofTable 1reports the fixed parameters. We normalize the steady state level of productivityA¯⁠, the mean of the firms’ idiosyncratic shocksE{zi}⁠, and the disutility of laborχto one. For several parameters, we use standard values from the literature.25We set the discount factorβof households andβmof managers to0.95(i.e., the risk-free rate is 5%in the nonstochastic steady state), the depreciationδto0.1⁠, the autocorrelation of productivity shocksρto0.95⁠, and the coefficientγof capital in the production function to0.3⁠. We set the risk aversion of managersγmto three, as inGlover and Levine (2015,2017), and the households’ risk aversionγcto three as well, which is in line with the values used in the literature (see, e.g.,Barro 2006). To discipline the parametersθf⁠,θe⁠, andθothat govern the manager compensation, we use executive compensation data from ExecuComp. FollowingDittmann and Maug (2007)andGlover and Levine (2017), we measure (a) the fixed pay as the sum of salary, bonus, other compensations, and long-term pay incentives as a fraction of total assets; (b) the shares of equity owned by CEOs; and (c) the options awarded to CEOs as a fraction of common shares outstanding (seeInternet Appendix B.1for more details). Because the manager’s choices in Proposition 1 depend on the relative importance of the fixed, equity, and option compensation only, we can normalize one of the parametersθf⁠,θe⁠,θoto one. We set the parameterγdthat governs households’ liquidity value from deposits to 1.4, which is the same value used byDavydiuk (2017)andBegenau (2020).

Panel B ofTable 1reports the parameters that we calibrate toward the indicated data moment. We focus most of the discussion on the full calibration with both firms’ and households’ deposits, and comment briefly at the end about the restricted calibration in which all the deposits are held by households.

We begin by calibrating the parameters that determine the deposits held by households and firms. Households’ and firms’ demand for deposits are determined byψand the distribution of the idiosyncratic shockszti⁠, respectively. As noted in Section3.1,ztican take valueszH⁠,zLwith probabilitespzand1−pz⁠, respectively. To measure these two quantities in the data, we proceed as follows. For deposits, we use data from the Flow of Funds (FF) and the Survey of Consumer Finances (SCF).27We find that households hold about two-thirds of the total deposits held jointly by households and firms (seeInternet Appendix B.2for more details). We cross-check the total deposits from the FF and SCF with data on deposits from the FDIC and the National Credit Union Administration, and the two measures are consistent with each other. We measure the liquidity premium in the data as the difference between the rate on 3-month AA commercial paper, which proxies safe illiquid assets as inDavydiuk (2017), and the rate paid by banks on deposits. We measure the latter as the ratio of interest expense on domestic deposits relative to the stock of such deposits, using FDIC data. This approach yields a liquidity premium of 1.12%per year (seeInternet Appendix B.3for more details).

The size of the banking sector in the model is affected by the ratio of bank lending,bt⁠, to physical capital owned by the firms,kt⁠. This ratio is governed by the borrowing constraint (2) and, in particular, the borrowing limitξ⁠. We setξ=0.3002to match the 0.43 deposits-to-GDP ratio in the data.

We set the parameter p z that governs firms’ probability of high and low idiosyncratic shocks to match the average employment growth rate of firms that expand their labor force, corresponding to firms that receive the high value of z t + 1 i in the model. We define the average employment growth rate as g t + 1 i ≡ l ^ t + 1 i − l t i 1 2 ( l ^ t + 1 i + l t i ) , where l t i = ϕ t l a t i and l ^ t + 1 i = ϕ t + 1 l a t + 1 i | z t + 1 i = z H ⁠ . Given the structure of our model, the growth rate g t + 1 i is independent of a t i ⁠ , so that the target does not depend on the distribution of firms’ assets. Haltiwanger, Jarmin, and Miranda (2013) show that for all but the youngest firms, this growth rate is around 2.5 % per year.

The parametersσ⁠,λ1⁠, andλ2govern banks’ default probability, as discussed in Section4.1. We discipline them using bank default rates, measured as the fraction of banks that are taken over by the FDIC (weighted by assets) and for which the FDIC incurred a positive resolution cost. The parameterλ1determines whether the risk-shifting constraintpt≥0is binding or not—when it is binding (i.e.,pt=0⁠), banks do not risk-shift and the probability of default is low, and when it does not (i.e.,pt>0⁠), banks engage in risk shifting and the default rate spikes. Thus, we setλ1to match the fraction of low- versus high-default years, which we define as follows. We sort the yearly default rates in the data from the lowest to the highest. We define high-default years to be (a) the 2008–2009 crisis and (b) all other years with a default rate higher than that. Item (b) implies that 1988–1992 (i.e., most of the Saving and Loan crisis) and 2010 are also high-default years.28Overall, 68%of the years in the sample are defined as low-default times, and we setλ1so that, in the model,pt=0and default is low 68%of the time. We obtain the restricted calibration in which all the deposits are held by households by shutting down the frictions in the production side of the model other than the need to have some of the physical capital financed by bank loans. As a result, our model is as close as possible toBegenau (2020)and other papers in the literature. In particular, (a) we shut down firms’ idiosyncratic risk by settingpz=1⁠, which implies that firms invest all their wealthatiin capital and households hold 100%of the deposits; (b) we setφ=0and select the remaining parameter values to match the same targets as in the full calibration; and (c) we replace managers’ marginal utility,Λtm⁠, with that of households,Λt⁠, to solve for firms’ labor demand in (10), so that managers have no independent role in firms’ decision-making.

4.3 Simulation and untargeted moments

Table 2lists nontargeted moments for both the full and restricted models, though in what follows we focus most of the discussion on the full calibration with both firms’ and households’ deposits.

The model matches several nontargeted moments well. These include business cycle moments (e.g., the volatility of investments and wages, and the correlation of GDP with investments, consumption, and lagged GDP) as well as several moments related to banks and deposits (e.g., volatility of bank profits and its correlation with GDP, the correlation between the deposit premium and deposits/labor, and the ratio of banks’ assets to the stock of capital). The fit is also good with respect to the volatility of households’ deposits, which is computed using one observation every 3 years because of data availability from the SCF.29

We emphasize that the model matches well several moments related to the default rate of banks, which is a central element of the bad risk-taking side of the model. These include the correlation of bank default rates with GDP growth, the volatility of bank default, and the average FDIC resolution costs relative to the assets of failed banks. We also compute the correlation between risk-weighted assets and the one-period-ahead bank default rate. In the model, as banks increase risk-shiftingpt>0⁠, risk-weighted assets go up at timetand the default rate goes up att+1⁠. This is the case in the data (correlation: 0.44), and the model matches this moment well. The fit is also good when we consider the ratio of risk-weighted to total assets—as opposed to just risk-weighted assets—which captures shifts toward riskier investments in the data for any given level of assets.

Although the model seems to miss a few untargeted moments, a careful inspection reveals that the model nonetheless performs relatively well. The volatility of the deposit premium is substantially lower than in the data, but this result is common in macro-banking models; see for instanceDavydiuk (2017). The correlation of firms’ deposits and labor with GDP are somewhat lower than in the data; however, when we consider the ratio of firms’ deposits to labor—motivated by the link between these two quantities in the main channel that affects our results—the data fit improves significantly with respect to the correlation with GDP, and the volatility is close to the data. The correlation between the deposit premium and wages is negative in the data and slightly positive in the model, but the correlation in the data is not statistically different from zero.

The fit of the restricted calibration in which households hold all the deposits is good in some dimensions, but not as good as the full calibration in others. However, if one keeps in mind that various features of the model are designed to produce accurate results in the full calibration with both firms’ and households’ deposits, then the performance of the restricted calibration appears relatively good.

4.4 Results

To compute the welfare effects of changing capital requirements, we begin by simulating an economy with an 8%capital requirement, a value in between the 7.26%used byDavydiuk (2017)and the 9.25%used byBegenau (2020). We then change the capital requirement to a new level,ζ⁠, and compute the welfare as the economy transitions to the new stochastic steady state.

The solid line inFigure 3represents welfare in consumption-equivalent percentage units for the full calibration in which both firms and households hold deposits. The optimal capital requirement in this economy is 21.9%. This is much higher than the optimal capital requirement in several models of the literature in which only households hold deposits, such as the 12.4%inBegenau (2020)and the 16%inBegenau and Landvoigt (2021).

The dotted line inFigure 3represents the welfare in the restricted calibration in which all the deposits are held by households. The optimal capital requirement in this calibration is 14.6%. This is substantially lower than the optimal requirement in the full calibration when firms hold deposits (i.e., 7.3 percentage points lower), though it is in line with the Basel III fully phased-in level of 14%–15%(Basel Committee on Banking Supervision 2017) as well as the optimum inBegenau and Landvoigt (2021)andBegenau (2020).

Figure 3reveals two key differences between the welfare functions in the two calibrations, in addition to the higher optimum obtained when firms’ hold deposits. First, the percent of consumption the household is willing to give up to transition from theζ=8%economy to the welfare-maximizing economy is an order of magnitude higher in the full calibration (0.224%of annual consumption vs. 0.028%⁠). Second, in the restricted calibration, the welfare function is nearly symmetric around the optimum, a feature shared by other models with only households’ deposits, such asBegenau (2020). But in the full calibration that includes firms’ deposits, the welfare function is almost flat for capital requirements higher than the optimum, which is again related to firms’ deposits and our novel channel. To quantify this difference, we note that the restricted calibration predicts that overshooting the optimal capital requirement by 11.4 percentage points reduces the welfare gains to about zero: households in the model without firm deposits are indifferent between leavingζat 8%and transitioning toζ=26.0%⁠. On the other hand, overshooting the optimal capital requirement by 11.4 percentage points in the full calibration leaves the welfare gain almost unchanged (0.198%when overshooting vs. 0.224%at the optimum). A capital requirement this high in the restricted calibration would actually reduce welfare.

The different shape of the welfare function in the full versus restricted calibrations is a robust feature of our model; it arises in all the alternative calibrations and quantitative exploration exercises we perform in Section4.5(seeInternet Appendix H). This result is relevant for practical policy implementation. Quantitative models in the capital requirement literature have produced different values for the optimal requirement, and in practice, a regulator might be concerned about the effects of undershooting vs. overshooting the true optimum. Our analysis suggests that it is better for a policymaker who is uncertain about the true optimum to overshoot the optimal requirement rather than undershoot it.

We interpret the values reported inTable 3as capturing the long-run effects of changes in capital requirements. First, changing capital requirements has large effects on banks’ financing (i.e., fewer deposits and more equity), a significant drop in the bank default rate, but only a small reduction in the size of the banking sector (as measured by total bank assets). The lower default rate is mainly driven by a reduction in risk shifting.

Second, firms’ long-run demand for deposits is much more price-elastic than households’ demand—in a sense, deposits are less important to firms than they are to households. Indeed, the deposit premium increases by only 11 basis points asζincreases to 21.9%—the same increase inζin the restricted calibration with only households’ deposits produces an increase of about 30 basis points, despite a similar reduction in deposits. The result can also be seen by noting that firms’ deposits drop much more than households’ deposits asζincreases.

Third, higher capital requirements lead to very small changes in real macroeconomic variables. In particular, the apparently small effect on wages does not contradict the intuition outlined in Section2. The reduction in firms’ deposits alone would have large effects on labor. However, firms’ labor demand is very elastic and, thus, a small drop in wages is sufficient to prevent a large drop in labor. We also note that (a) the effect of increasing capital requirements on wages in the restricted calibration with only households’ deposits is roughly four times smaller than the effect reported here, and (b) the robustness analysis in Section4.5further supports the intuition of Section2by showing the importance of two key labor market parameters: the Frisch elasticity of labor supplyηand the degree of wage rigiditiesω⁠. In addition, the improvement in regulation gives rise to a wealth effect that reduces households’ labor supply and counteracts the drop in wages driven by lower labor demand.30

Finally, while our analysis uses a representative household, a concern in practice is that the reduction in wages and labor could have a disproportionate impact on households that derive most of their income from labor. While a full analysis with households’ heterogeneity is outside the scope of this paper,Table 3shows that the labor share of income is hardly affected, dropping by only 0.29%⁠, which is an order of magnitude smaller than the 5 percentage point drop documented byKarabarbounis and Neiman (2014)related to the decline in the price of investment goods.

First, we consider a calibration based on a lower liquidity premium on deposits. We follow the approach of some of the papers in the capital requirement literature that assume that bank deposits are close substitutes with Treasury securities (see, e.g.,Davydiuk 2017). That is, we compute the liquidity premium on Treasury securities, which is 0.56%in our sample, and use this target for the deposit premium. The 0.56%premium is computed followingKrishnamurthy and Vissing-Jorgensen (2012)and adjusting their sample to match ours (seeInternet Appendix B.3).32The calibration matches the lower liquidity premium by changing the parametersψandzLin a way that reduces the demand for deposits, while holding fixed the other targets, in particular, the ratio of households’ to firms’ deposits. With a lower demand, the cost of raising capital requirements drops and, thus, the optimal capital requirement is higher. Nonetheless, the gap between the full and restricted calibrations remains substantial at almost 3 percentage points.

Next, we consider a simple extension in which firms can invest not only in deposits but also in other liquid assets, such as Treasury securities.33With this extension, the optimal capital requirement is 22.5%and thus very close to the 21.9% baseline.34This is because, when we add other liquid assets, we recalibrate the model to match the other targets and, in particular, the liquidity premium. This is achieved by increasing firms’ demand for liquid assets, which offsets the direct effects of the additional supply of liquid assets.

To illustrate our main model mechanism, we solve the model with both a higher and a lower Frisch elasticity:η=1.5andη=0.5⁠. The higherηexceeds the maximum values used in the recent macro-labor literature (Dyrda, Kaplan, and Ríos-Rull 2012), and the lowerηis approximately the lower bound implied byChetty et al. (2011,2013). The optimal capital requirement gap between the full and restricted calibrations weakens withη=1.5but remains large at 3.8 percentage points. Forη=0.5⁠, the gap widens to 10.2 percentage points. These results are in line with the theoretical discussion in Section2.

We then turn to the degree of wage rigidities. The baseline calibration matches the volatility of wages relative to GDP (=0.77), but we also consider alternative measures of wages in the data and find that [0.73, 0.80] represents a reasonable range for this moment (seeInternet Appendix B.5). We thus conduct two robustness exercises in which we replace the target for the volatility of employment with the lower and upper bound for the volatility of wages, respectively, which we match by varyingω⁠. The difference between the full and restricted calibration changes to 2.0%and 9.8%with more and less wage rigidities, respectively, in line with the discussion in Section2.

Next, we study the role of managers’ preferences and compensation, which affect the optimum in the full calibration but do not play a role in the restricted one without firms’ deposits. First, we consider a version of the model in which the manager has Epstein-Zin (EZ) preferences (seeInternet Appendix E), setting the additional EZ parameter to obtain an intertemporal elasticity of substitution of 1.5 (Beeler and Campbell 2012). Second, we reduce the risk aversionγmof managers to 2 (as inPage 2018). We then change the relative importance of the fixed and option compensation of the manager—first setting them to zero, and then doubling their weight relative to the equity compensation. The optimal capital requirement is nearly unchanged or higher, thereby confirming and strengthening our results.

We then varyγd⁠, which affects the elasticity of households’ demand for deposits and whose value could affect the optimal regulation (see the discussion inBegenau 2020). We reduceγdfrom 1.4 to 0.8 to illustrate the effect of this change. The optimal capital requirement is higher in both the full and restricted calibrations, but the gap between the two optima remains large (i.e., 5.9 percentage points).

We also extend the model to allow for a deadweight loss from default by assuming that a fractionϖof assetsbt−1of banks that default attis lost. We setϖ=4.5%by proxying deadweight losses with the weighted average of total receivership expenses for banks taken over by the FDIC (Bennett and Unal 2015, table 3). With this additional cost of default, the benefits of raising capital requirements are higher—and more so in the full calibration—thereby strengthening our result and increasing the gap to 8.8 percentage points.

We then conduct an illustrative analysis by increasing the fraction of deposits held by households from 66.4%to 82.8%⁠, which corresponds to the fraction in 1989, and is the highest in our sample (seeInternet Appendix B.2). The optimal capital requirement increases from 21.9% to 24.5%⁠, and because the optimum is unchanged in the calibration with only households deposits, the gap that originates from our channel widens to 9.9 percentage points. Though it may appear puzzling that reducing firms’ deposits actually increases the strength of a channel that works through firms’ deposits, we note that with more households’ deposits, we have to reduce the dividend adjustment cost parameterφto match the volatility of firms’ deposits. A lowerφimplies that firms’ demand for deposits is much more elastic. Thus, as regulators increase capital requirements, the reduction in deposits is absorbed relatively more by firms, and our novel channel plays a bigger role.

Finally, we investigate the role of bad risk-taking by shutting down banks’ risk shifting. We do so by settingλ1high enough so that banks always choosept=0⁠.35Without risk shifting, the model does not produce the large spikes in banks’ default rate that we observe in the data—there are no more banking crises—and thus this exercise substantially reduces the benefit of tightening capital requirements. In this sense, this exercise can be viewed as a way to illustrate the model mechanisms. The optimal capital requirement in the full calibration is 3.2%⁠, which is 1.3 percentage points higher than in the restricted calibration. While this gap is small in absolute value, it remains quite large when compared to the level of the optimal capital requirement in this robustness exercise.
article.5. Deposits and Good Risk-Taking: Indepe:
In this section we provide independent evidence that supports the core mechanism of our model. We have shown that adding firms’ deposits to a quantitative dynamic general equilibrium leads to an optimal capital requirement that is higher than in a model in which all deposits are held by households, and that this result is driven by the good risk-taking channel. That is, (a) regulation affects banks’ supply of deposits; (b) firms respond by changing their good risk-taking actions; and (c) firms’ response is dampened by a general equilibrium effect which operates through wages. The main challenge with providing independent evidence for the good risk-taking channel is to find exogenous variation in the supply of deposits. We address this challenge in two ways.

In Section5.1, we replicate and extend an analysis fromDrechsler, Savov, and Schnabl (2017)to show that exogenous changes in deposits affect employment and wages, controlling for the effects that are transmitted directly through bank lending. As inDrechsler, Savov, and Schnabl (2017), we exploit the fact that deposits drop more in counties with higher deposit concentration, following a rate hike by the Federal Reserve.

In a second approach, described inInternet Appendix F, we use the staggered implementation of interstate banking deregulation as an instrument for a change in deposits, followingJayaratne and Strahan (1996),Mian, Sufi, and Verner (2020), and a large literature that has employed this instrument. We find that deregulation triggers an increase in deposits, which in turn has a positive effect on employment and wages. While this analysis represents a less direct way to establish evidence for the good risk-taking channel, it is nonetheless consistent with it, thereby providing additional support.

Overall, these results are consistent with the novel mechanism in our model and support the good risk-taking channel, and they are in line with other studies that have looked directly at the effects of safe asset holdings on firm-level employment, such asBenmelech, Bergman, and Seru (2021).

5.1 The deposit channel of monetary policy and good risk-taking

To provide support for the good risk-taking channel, we build on the approach ofDrechsler, Savov, and Schnabl (2017)(henceforth DSS). DSS show that when the Fed raises interest rates, banks widen the spreads they charge on deposits, and deposits flow out of the banking system. Crucially, this effect is stronger in areas in which banks have more market power, which DSS measure using the county-level deposit Herfindahl Hirschman Index (HHI). They label this result the deposit channel of monetary policy.

We build on DSS and use the cross-sectional dispersion in the county-level deposit HHI to generate variation in how deposits respond to changes in monetary policy. The deposit channel of monetary policy predicts that counties in which banks have more market power (i.e., a higher HHI) experience a larger drop of deposits when the Fed funds rate increases. Our objective is to check whether such counties also experience a larger drop in employment and wages, as predicted by the good risk-taking channel. As a crucial step, we can exploit a feature of the deposit channel of DSS to control for the effects on employment and wages that are attributable to changes in bank lending, thus isolating the good risk-taking channel from other channels through which banking shocks affect employment and wages.

We combine the data used by DSS on (a) county-level concentration in the deposit market, (b) Fed funds rates, and (c) county-level employment with (d) county-year-level wages from the March Supplement of the Current Population Survey. We construct an annual panel at the county-year level from 1997 to 2013. Our baseline regression is y c , t = α c + α t + β Δ F F t × H H I c + ε c , t , (33) where y c , t is the logarithm of the growth in employment or the change in wages in county c and time t ⁠ , F F t is the Fed funds rate at time t ⁠ , and H H I c is the deposit Herfindahl index that measures concentration in the deposit market in county c ⁠ . We construct H H I c as described in DSS. Table 5 presents the estimate of our baseline regression. Columns 1-3 contain the results about employment, and columns 4-6 those about wages.

Column 1 ofTable 5shows that fed funds shocks reduce employment more in counties with higher deposit concentration; column 4 shows that wages also respond more to fed funds shocks in counties with higher deposit concentration. These two results, combined with the central insight of DSS, suggest that a reduction in deposits reduces labor demand, consistent with the good risk-taking channel. Columns 2 and 5 add county fixed effects interacted with a dummy for the zero-lower-bound period, following DSS, and the results are essentially unchanged.

The results in columns 1–2 and 4–5 ofTable 5are consistent with the good risk-taking channel, but they also may be driven by a standard bank-lending channel. That is, fed funds shocks might reduce aggregate lending demand overall, generating a reduction in labor demand by firms. To control for the effects of bank lending, we build on another strategy developed by DSS, who conjecture that counties with banks that experience larger outflows of deposits due to the deposit channel—and that do so not just in countycbutacross all the counties they operate—reduce new lending (table VII, columns 1 and 2 in DSS). They show that this is indeed the case by constructing a proxy for the deposit outflow; columns 3 and 6 include the same proxy in our regression and show that our results are robust to this control.

Although column 3 ofTable 5is the same as one of the regressions in DSS (in particular, the same as table VII, column 4 in DSS), our purpose in estimating it is different.36DSS are interested in studying how the deposit channel affects the labor market through lending, controlling for the direct effects of deposits. For us, it is the opposite: we want to understand the effects of deposits on the labor market, controlling for lending. To that end, the additional results in columns 4–6 that the reduction in deposits also reduces wages provides additional evidence in favor of the good risk-taking channel.
article.6. Conclusion:
We study capital requirement regulation when both households and firms hold deposits. Because of a novel general equilibrium effect related to firms’ deposits and wages, the optimal capital requirement is substantially higher than in comparable models—used in most of the literature—in which all the deposits are held by households.

To keep our model tractable and comparable to most of the literature on optimal capital requirements, we assumed that all households are alike and all banks are alike. We acknowledge, however, that departing from these modeling assumptions would give rise to additional policy implications. For instance, households in our model are both workers and owners of the firms; in a model in which workers and owners are separate agents, a change in capital requirements that alters wages and firms’ profits might give rise to policy-relevant redistributive effects. In addition, all the banks in our model cater equally to firms and households; if banks instead specialize in serving particular agents, we conjecture that it might be optimal to impose a looser capital requirement on banks that serve households and a tighter one on those that cater more to firms’ deposits.

This paper opens up several directions for future research. First, depending on how firms’ idiosyncratic productivities evolve over time, the value of deposits to firms might be time varying. This effect might give rise to important implications for the analysis of time-varying regulation above and beyond those analyzed byDavydiuk (2017)andMalherbe (2020), in which the value of deposits is constant over time and time variation in optimal capital requirements comes from time variation in banks’ overinvestment. Second, we have followed the literature in assuming complete deposit insurance, butEgan, Hortaçsu, and Matvos (2017)note that only about half of all deposits in the United States are in fact FDIC insured. Our model can be employed to study the optimal degree of deposit insurance. In our model, eliminating deposit insurance altogether increases firms’ cash flow volatility, and thus the optimal outcome is likely to be partial deposit insurance.

Authors have furnished an Internet Appendix, which is available on the Oxford University Press Web site next to the link to the final published paper online.
